1.3  Purpose & Scope

This Product Specification Manual provides the authoritative technical reference for four selected dredger

models from the Benamdaj Dredging Solutions Ltd. fleet. It serves customer support, pre-sales engineering,

maintenance planning and spare parts identification.

Dredger

The 16/14 Inch Cutter Suction Dredger is the highest-capacity vessel in this documented series, designed for

high-volume dredging in rivers, harbours, and coastal waterways. Powered by a Weichai 6170 series marine

diesel engine transmitting power to the dredge pump via a marine gearbox, with a Caterpillar 3412 auxiliary

engine for onboard electrical systems. The vessel uses a jet suction system and is positioned by a manual

anchor winch arrangement with 4 anchor points.

Multifunctional Dredger

The 14/12 Inch Amphibious Multifunctional Dredger combines dredging capability with amphibious mobility,

enabling operation in shallow water, marshes, and areas inaccessible to conventional hull-mounted dredgers.

Powered by a Weichai 6170 series engine and marine gearbox, it achieves a maximum in-situ production rate

of 300 m3/hr.

Product Specification: 10/10 Inch Jet Suction Dredger

The 10/10 Inch Jet Suction Dredger is the most compact vessel in the Benamdaj fleet and the only model

driven by a PTO (Power Take-Off) system rather than a marine gearbox. Powered by a Weichai WP13 marine

diesel engine, it achieves a maximum dredging depth of 18 m and a discharge distance of 300 m. A

Caterpillar 3412 auxiliary engine provides onboard electrical power, consistent with all other fleet models.

Operations & Maintenance Manual   |   CONFIDENTIAL

heading: 1.  Introduction & Scope
text:
1.  Introduction & Scope

This Operations and Maintenance Manual provides comprehensive guidance for the safe operation and

routine maintenance of four dredger models operated by Benamdaj Dredging Solutions Ltd. It covers pre-start

procedures, startup and shutdown sequences, daily and periodic maintenance schedules, operating

guidelines, and emergency procedures.

This manual applies equally to all four vessels in the documented series: the 16/14" Cutter Suction Dredger,

the 14/12" Amphibious Multifunctional Dredger, the 12/10" Bucket Chain Dredger, and the 10/10" Jet Suction

Dredger. Where procedures differ between models this is clearly noted. All dredgers share the same electrical

system (CAT 3412 auxiliary), the same 240 V manual control configuration, and the same winch-based

positioning system.

Benamdaj Dredging Solutions Ltd.  |  Marine Engineering  |  BDS-OM-001  |  Ver 1.0  |  Page 2  |  Uncontrolled when printed

|  |
 | NOTE: If any item on this checklist cannot be confirmed satisfactory, do not start the engine. Report the issue |
 | to the supervisor before proceeding. |
 |  |

|  |  |  |  |
1 |  |  |  | Check engine oil level using the dipstick. Level must be between the MIN and MAX marks. Top up |
 |  |  |  | with Total Ruby X if below MIN. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
2 |  |  |  | Check fuel level in diesel tank. Plan refuelling before the shift if level is below half. Refuel only with the |
 |  |  |  | engine stopped. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
3 |  |  |  | Inspect all hose connections, pipe joints, and suction line fittings for signs of leakage. Tighten or |
 |  |  |  | replace as needed before starting. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |

2.  Pre-Start Safety Checklist

The following checklist must be completed every morning before the engine is started and before

commencing any dredging shift. All items must be confirmed satisfactory before the start sequence is initiated.

3.  Engine Startup Sequence

Follow these steps in order every time the engine is started. Do not skip steps or vary the sequence.

heading: 3.2  Pump & Suction System Engagement
text:
3.2  Pump & Suction System Engagement

After the main engine has warmed up and all gauges confirm normal operation:

Benamdaj Dredging Solutions Ltd.  |  Marine Engineering  |  BDS-OM-001  |  Ver 1.0  |  Page 4  |  Uncontrolled when printed

|  |
 | WARNING: Never shut down the engine without first flushing the pump. Slurry left in the pump and |
 | pipes will solidify and cause blockage or premature wear. |
 |  |

|  |  |  |  |
 | 1 |  |  | Reduce engine speed to idle. Stop dredging swing by securing anchor winches. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
2 |  |  |  | Open clean water flushing supply to pump suction (or submerge suction in clear water area). Run |
 |  |  |  | pump for minimum 5 minutes to flush all slurry from pump casing, impeller, and discharge pipe. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
3 |  |  |  | Raise suction ladder/arm to the transport/stowed position using the manual winch. Secure with locking |
 |  |  |  | pin or rope. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | 4 |  |  | Disengage pump drive by disengaging gearbox (Models 1-3) or PTO (Model 4). |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | 5 |  |  | Reduce engine speed to idle. Allow 3-5 minutes cool-down at idle before shutdown. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | 6 |  |  | Turn ignition key to OFF. Confirm engine has fully stopped. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | 7 |  |  | Switch off auxiliary engine (CAT 3412). Confirm all electrical loads are disconnected. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | 8 |  |  | Secure all anchor wires and mooring lines. Confirm vessel is correctly moored for overnight. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | 9 |  |  | Remove and relocate any dredged material (sediments/mud) from the work area as required. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
10 |  |  |  | Complete the end-of-day operational log -- record engine hours, production volume, and any faults or |
 |  |  |  | incidents. |
 |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |
1 |  |  |  | Check engine oil level after shutdown and top up if needed -- oil level drops slightly when engine is |
 |  |  |  | hot. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
2 |  |  |  | Inspect pump seal/gland for leakage and record condition. Replace seal packing if excessive drip is |
 |  |  |  | observed. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | 3 |  |  | Inspect all hose connections and pipe joints for leaks that developed during the shift. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | 4 |  |  | Check anchor wires and winch drums -- inspect for kinks, fraying, or uneven spooling. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | 5 |  |  | Confirm fire extinguisher is back in its designated location. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | 6 |  |  | Report any defects, unusual noises, or production issues to the supervisor before leaving the vessel. |
 |  |  |  |  |

1.  Introduction & How to Use This Guide

This Troubleshooting Guide covers all four Benamdaj dredger models: the 16/14" Cutter Suction Dredger

(Weichai 6170 engine, marine gearbox), the 14/12" Amphibious Multifunctional Dredger (Weichai 6170,

marine gearbox), the 12/10" Bucket Chain Dredger (CAT 3408, marine gearbox), and the 10/10" Jet Suction

Dredger (Weichai WP13, PTO drive). All models use a CAT 3412 auxiliary engine for electrical generation.

Procedures in this guide are largely identical across models since all four share the same winch-based

manual positioning, the same electrical system, and the same high-chrome centrifugal slurry pump type.

Where a procedure differs by model this is explicitly noted.

HOW TO USE:  (1) Find the symptom in the Quick Reference index at Section 2. (2) Go to the relevant

fault block. (3) Follow the diagnostic steps in order. (4) Apply the fix. (5) Apply the prevention steps to

avoid recurrence.

heading: SYMPTOM: Engine will not start |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Engine will not start |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Insufficient fuel -- tank empty or fuel valve closed. 2. Flat or failed |
 |  |  |  | battery. 3. Engine oil below minimum level -- safety cut-out activated. 4. |
 |  |  |  | Clogged fuel filter. 5. Faulty ignition switch. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Check fuel gauge first. Then check battery terminals (clean and tight?). |
 |  |  |  | Check oil dipstick. Try cranking -- if starter motor sounds weak, battery is |
 |  |  |  | low. If starter motor does not engage, check ignition fuse. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Refuel if empty. 2. Charge or replace battery. 3. Top up engine oil to |
 |  |  |  | correct level. 4. Replace fuel filter. 5. Check ignition fuse and replace if |
 |  |  |  | blown. 6. If none of the above resolves it, call Level 2 technician. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Fuel filter, engine oil (Total Ruby X), battery, ignition fuse |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners, voltmeter, funnel |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | 15-45 minutes for operator-level fixes |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 (operator) for fuel, battery, oil. Level 2 for fuel filter, ignition fault. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Check oil, fuel, and battery at every pre-start checklist. Replace fuel filter at |
 |  |  |  | every 200-hour service. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | No -- routine consumables are not warranty items. |
 |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |
 |

heading: SYMPTOM: Engine starts then immediately stalls |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Engine starts then immediately stalls |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Fuel starvation -- blocked fuel filter or air in fuel line. 2. Idle speed set too |
 |  |  |  | low. 3. Engine not warmed up before loading. 4. Water in fuel. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Check if engine runs smoothly at idle without load. If stalls only under load: |
 |  |  |  | check gearbox engagement and pump back-pressure. If stalls at idle: check |
 |  |  |  | fuel filter and idle adjustment. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Replace fuel filter and bleed fuel system. 2. Adjust idle speed to |
 |  |  |  | manufacturer's minimum (confirm with engine manual). 3. Drain water from |
 |  |  |  | fuel separator. 4. If engine runs without gearbox engaged but stalls when |
 |  |  |  | pump is loaded -- check pump for mechanical blockage. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Fuel filter, fuel separator element |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners, screwdriver, bleed kit |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | 30-60 minutes |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for idle and fuel. Level 2 for injector or pump issues. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Replace fuel filter every 200 hours. Use clean, dry diesel fuel storage. Drain |
 |  |  |  | water separator weekly. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | No -- fuel system maintenance is operator responsibility. |
 |  |  |  |  |

|  |

3.  Engine & Gearbox Faults

The following fault blocks apply to all models. Note that Models 1, 2, 3 use a marine gearbox between engine

and pump. Model 4 uses a PTO (Power Take-Off) instead -- gearbox-specific faults do not apply to Model 4.

Benamdaj Dredging Solutions Ltd.  |  Marine Engineering  |  BDS-TSG-001  |  Ver 1.0  |  Page 4  |  Uncontrolled when printed

|  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Cooling fan blocked or not operating. 2. Engine overloaded -- running at |
 |  |  |  | excessive RPM for extended period. 3. Low engine oil causing increased |
 |  |  |  | friction heat. 4. Blocked air intake. 5. Engine room restricted airflow. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Stop engine immediately when alarm sounds. Allow to cool 10 minutes. |
 |  |  |  | Check cooling fan rotation. Check oil level. Check air intake for blockage. |
 |  |  |  | Check engine compartment ventilation. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Clear any blockage from cooling fan and air intake. 2. Check and top up |
 |  |  |  | engine oil. 3. Reduce engine speed -- do not run at maximum RPM for |
 |  |  |  | extended periods in hot ambient conditions. 4. Ensure engine compartment |
 |  |  |  | has adequate ventilation. 5. If overheating recurs, call Level 2 technician. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Engine oil (Total Ruby X) |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | Cool-down 10-15 minutes. Fix 15-30 minutes. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for oil top-up, blockage clearing. Level 2 for fan motor failure. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Check oil level and air intake every day. Maintain engine compartment |
 |  |  |  | ventilation. Do not run engine above rated RPM for extended periods. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | Depends on cause -- overloading or lack of oil maintenance voids warranty. |
 |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |
 |

heading: SYMPTOM: Low engine oil pressure warning |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Low engine oil pressure warning |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Engine oil level below minimum. 2. Engine oil pump failure. 3. Blocked oil |
 |  |  |  | filter. 4. Oil leak -- external or internal. 5. Oil viscosity incorrect for ambient |
 |  |  |  | temperature. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | STOP ENGINE IMMEDIATELY. Low oil pressure causes rapid bearing |
 |  |  |  | failure. Check oil level on dipstick. Inspect underside of engine for oil leak. |
 |  |  |  | Check oil filter condition. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Top up engine oil to MAX mark and recheck pressure. 2. Replace oil filter |
 |  |  |  | at next service if blocked. 3. If external oil leak is visible, locate and seal |
 |  |  |  | source. 4. If oil level is correct but pressure is still low -- call Level 3 |
 |  |  |  | specialist (internal oil pump or bearing failure). |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Engine oil (Total Ruby X), oil filter |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners, oil drain tray |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Estimated repair time |  |  |  | Oil top-up: 10 minutes. Seal leak: 1-3 hours. Internal failure: specialist |
 |  |  |  | required. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for oil top-up. Level 3 for internal pressure failure. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Check oil level every day before startup. Change oil and filter every 200 |
 |  |  |  | hours. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | Failure due to running without oil voids warranty. |
 |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |
 |

heading: SYMPTOM: Engine loses power under load |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Engine loses power under load |  |  |
 |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  | 1. Clogged air filter restricting combustion air. 2. Clogged fuel filter |
 |  |  | restricting fuel supply. 3. Pump back-pressure too high -- slurry too dense |
 |  |  | or discharge blocked. 4. Engine RPM not set to rated operating speed. 5. |
 |  |  | Engine operating above its rated continuous duty power. |
 |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  | Check air filter -- hold up to light, replace if blocked. Check fuel filter. Check |
 |  |  | pump pressure gauge -- if above 10 bar, reduce RPM and investigate |
 |  |  | discharge line. Confirm engine RPM is within 1,200-2,100 range. |
 |  |  |  |

Troubleshooting Guide   |   CONFIDENTIAL

Benamdaj Dredging Solutions Ltd.  |  Marine Engineering  |  BDS-TSG-001  |  Ver 1.0  |  Page 5  |  Uncontrolled when printed

|  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Replace air filter. 2. Replace fuel filter. 3. Clear discharge pipe if blocked. |
 |  |  |  | 4. Reduce pump load -- raise suction arm slightly to reduce density. 5. |
 |  |  |  | Allow engine cool-down if thermal derate is suspected. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Air filter, fuel filter |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | Filter replacement: 20-30 minutes |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for filters and pump adjustment. Level 2 for fuel injection issues. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Replace air and fuel filters every 200 hours. Monitor pump pressure |
 |  |  |  | continuously. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | No -- filter maintenance is operator responsibility. |
 |  |  |  |  |

heading: SYMPTOM: Excessive black smoke from exhaust |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Excessive black smoke from exhaust |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Clogged air filter -- insufficient combustion air causing rich mixture. 2. |
 |  |  |  | Engine severely overloaded. 3. Injector fault -- fuel not atomising correctly. |
 |  |  |  | 4. Engine running in very high ambient temperature. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Check air filter first -- this is the most common cause. If filter is clean, check |
 |  |  |  | engine load -- reduce RPM and see if smoke clears. If smoke continues at |
 |  |  |  | low load, call Level 2 for injector check. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Replace air filter immediately. 2. Reduce engine load. 3. Level 2: injector |
 |  |  |  | cleaning or replacement. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Air filter, injector set (Level 2) |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners, injector tool (Level 2) |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | Air filter: 20 minutes. Injectors: 2-4 hours (Level 2) |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for air filter. Level 2 for injectors. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Replace air filter every 200 hours. Inspect weekly in dusty/sandy |
 |  |  |  | environments. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | Injector failure: may be covered under warranty if within service life. |
 |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |
 |

heading: SYMPTOM: Unusual knocking or rattling from engine |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Unusual knocking or rattling from engine |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Low engine oil -- bearing knock. 2. Loose engine mounting bolts. 3. |
 |  |  |  | Damaged impeller contacting pump casing (transmitted vibration). 4. Faulty |
 |  |  |  | injector causing detonation knock. 5. Loose exhaust manifold. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | STOP ENGINE if knocking is severe. Check oil level. Check engine |
 |  |  |  | mounting bolts. Listen to isolate source -- is noise from engine, gearbox, or |
 |  |  |  | pump? Remove pump drive to determine if engine or pump is source. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Top up oil and recheck. 2. Tighten engine mounting bolts to correct |
 |  |  |  | torque. 3. If pump-source: inspect impeller for damage. 4. Level 2/3: internal |
 |  |  |  | engine knock requires specialist. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Engine oil, bolts, impeller (if pump source) |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Torque wrench, spanners |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | Oil and bolts: 30 minutes. Internal engine: specialist required. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for oil and bolts. Level 3 for internal knock. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Check oil daily. Check mounting bolts monthly. Replace impeller on |
 |  |  |  | schedule. |
 |  |  |  |  |

Troubleshooting Guide   |   CONFIDENTIAL

|  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | Internal knock due to oil starvation voids warranty. |
 |  |  |  |  |

heading: SYMPTOM: Gearbox overheating |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Gearbox overheating |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Low gearbox oil level. 2. Wrong grade of gearbox oil. 3. Gearbox |
 |  |  |  | overloaded -- pump back-pressure too high. 4. Prolonged operation without |
 |  |  |  | adequate cool-down. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Check gearbox oil level and condition every morning. If gearbox is hot to |
 |  |  |  | touch after normal operation: check oil level, check pump pressure for |
 |  |  |  | overload. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Top up gearbox oil to correct level with correct grade. 2. Allow cool-down. |
 |  |  |  | 3. Reduce pump back-pressure -- check for discharge blockage. 4. If |
 |  |  |  | overheating persists: Level 2 inspection of gearbox internals. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Gearbox oil (confirm grade with manufacturer) |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners, oil drain tray |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | Oil top-up: 15 minutes. Cool-down: 20-30 minutes. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for oil and cool-down. Level 2 for persistent overheating. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Check gearbox oil every morning. Change every 200 hours. Never exceed |
 |  |  |  | pump pressure limits. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | Damage from running with low oil or wrong oil grade voids warranty. |
 |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |
 |

heading: SYMPTOM: Gearbox oil leak |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Gearbox oil leak |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Worn or damaged shaft seal. 2. Loose drain plug or inspection cover. 3. |
 |  |  |  | Cracked gearbox casing from impact. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Identify leak location -- is it from shaft seal, drain plug, or casing? Check oil |
 |  |  |  | level to determine severity of loss. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Tighten drain plug or inspection cover. 2. Level 2: replace shaft seal -- |
 |  |  |  | requires gearbox dismantling. 3. Level 3: casing crack requires specialist or |
 |  |  |  | replacement. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Shaft seal kit, gasket set, drain plug washer |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners, seal driver, torque wrench |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | Drain plug: 15 minutes. Seal replacement: 2-4 hours (Level 2). |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for drain plug. Level 2 for seal. Level 3 for casing. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Check gearbox for leaks every morning. Replace shaft seal at major |
 |  |  |  | overhaul (90-day service). |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Warranty impact |  |  |  | Seal wear is normal wear -- not typically warranty. Impact damage may be |
 |  |  |  | assessed case by case. |
 |  |  |  |  |

Troubleshooting Guide   |   CONFIDENTIAL

heading: SYMPTOM: Pump loses suction / cavitates |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Pump loses suction / cavitates |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Suction head not sufficiently submerged -- pump drawing air. 2. Air |
 |  |  |  | ingress through suction pipe joint or fitting. 3. Slurry density too high -- |
 |  |  |  | pump cannot maintain suction. 4. Suction pipe partially blocked. 5. Worn |
 |  |  |  | impeller reducing suction efficiency. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Signs of cavitation: pressure gauge drops, pump output reduces, loud |
 |  |  |  | rattling noise from pump. Check: is suction head submerged? Check suction |
 |  |  |  | pipe fittings for air ingress. Check pressure reading. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Lower suction arm until head is fully submerged. 2. Check and tighten all |
 |  |  |  | suction pipe joints. 3. Reduce pump RPM to reduce slurry density entering |
 |  |  |  | pump. 4. Adjust system pressure to maintain pressure above cavitation |
 |  |  |  | threshold. 5. If impeller worn -- Level 2 replacement. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Suction pipe fittings, pipe clamps, impeller (if worn) |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners, pressure gauge |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Estimated repair time |  |  |  | Repositioning: 10-15 minutes. Seal: 30 minutes. Impeller: 4-8 hours (Level |
 |  |  |  | 2). |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for depth and fittings. Level 2 for impeller replacement. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Monitor pump pressure continuously. Replace impeller every 200 hours or |
 |  |  |  | when output reduces noticeably. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Warranty impact |  |  |  | Cavitation damage to impeller from running dry or incorrect operation may |
 |  |  |  | void warranty. |
 |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |
 |

heading: SYMPTOM: Pump output drops suddenly during dredging |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Pump output drops suddenly during dredging |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Suction pipe blockage -- large object or debris. 2. Discharge pipe |
 |  |  |  | blockage. 3. Worn impeller -- gradual loss of efficiency. 4. Engine RPM |
 |  |  |  | dropped -- check engine. 5. Suction head lifted off dredging face. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Check pump pressure -- if pressure drops: suction blockage. If pressure |
 |  |  |  | rises: discharge blockage. Check engine RPM. Check suction head |
 |  |  |  | position. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Suction blockage: raise arm from obstruction using winch, lower back and |
 |  |  |  | restart. 2. Discharge blockage: stop pump, loosen pipe joints to locate and |
 |  |  |  | clear blockage. 3. Worn impeller: Level 2 replacement. 4. Restore engine |
 |  |  |  | RPM. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Impeller, pipe couplings |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners, pipe wrench |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Estimated repair time |  |  |  | Repositioning: 15 minutes. Pipe clearance: 30-60 minutes. Impeller: Level |
 |  |  |  | 2. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for repositioning and basic blockage. Level 2 for impeller. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Monitor pressure continuously. Replace impeller on schedule. Inspect |
 |  |  |  | discharge pipe joints weekly. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | Normal wear items -- not warranty covered. |
 |  |  |  |  |

Troubleshooting Guide   |   CONFIDENTIAL

4.  Pump & Suction System Faults

The pump is the most critical component for dredging output. Reduced pump performance directly reduces

production. Monitor pump pressure (normal: 3 bar, max: 10 bar) continuously during operation.

heading: SYMPTOM: No flow from discharge pipe |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: No flow from discharge pipe |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Pump not primed -- air locked in suction line. 2. Complete discharge pipe |
 |  |  |  | blockage. 3. Pump drive not engaged -- gearbox or PTO not in drive. 4. |
 |  |  |  | Suction head not submerged. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Confirm pump drive is engaged and engine is running. Check suction head |
 |  |  |  | is submerged. Check discharge pipe for blockage starting at pump outlet. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Prime pump: ensure suction is submerged and restart pump at slow |
 |  |  |  | speed. 2. Clear discharge: loosen pipe couplings to locate blockage, flush |
 |  |  |  | with clean water. 3. Engage gearbox or PTO correctly. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Pipe couplings |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners, pipe wrench |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | Priming: 10-15 minutes. Discharge blockage: 30-90 minutes. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Always prime pump before starting. Flush discharge pipe with clean water at |
 |  |  |  | end of every shift. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | N/A -- operational issue. |
 |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |
 |

heading: SYMPTOM: Pump seal / gland leaking -- slurry from gland |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Pump seal / gland leaking -- slurry from gland |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Worn gland packing -- normal wear after extended operation. 2. |
 |  |  |  | Excessive suction pressure forcing slurry through gland. 3. Shaft scored or |
 |  |  |  | worn at seal location. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Inspect gland area during operation. A slow drip (2-3 drops per minute) is |
 |  |  |  | acceptable. Steady flow or slurry spray indicates immediate attention |
 |  |  |  | needed. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Tighten gland follower nuts gradually -- do not overtighten. 2. If still |
 |  |  |  | leaking: replace gland packing (Level 2). 3. If shaft is scored: Level 3 shaft |
 |  |  |  | repair or replacement. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Gland packing rope (confirm size with pump manufacturer) |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners, gland follower tool |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | Gland tightening: 15 minutes. Packing replacement: 2-4 hours (Level 2). |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for tightening. Level 2 for packing replacement. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Inspect gland at every shift. Tighten gland follower regularly. Replace |
 |  |  |  | packing at 200-hour service. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | Normal wear -- not warranty covered. |
 |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |
 |

heading: SYMPTOM: Suction pipe blocked |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Suction pipe blocked |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Large object (rock, debris, root) lodged in suction pipe or at suction head. |
 |  |  |  | 2. Solidified sediment if pump was shut down without flushing. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Pump pressure drops to zero with engine running. No discharge flow. |
 |  |  |  | Sound of pump running empty. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Raise suction arm away from dredging face using manual winch. 2. If |
 |  |  |  | blockage is at head: reposition and restart. 3. If blockage is in pipe: loosen |
 |  |  |  | couplings to locate and remove obstruction manually. 4. Flush with clean |
 |  |  |  | water before restarting. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Pipe couplings |
 |  |  |  |  |

Troubleshooting Guide   |   CONFIDENTIAL

Benamdaj Dredging Solutions Ltd.  |  Marine Engineering  |  BDS-TSG-001  |  Ver 1.0  |  Page 10  |  Uncontrolled when printed

|  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners, pipe wrench |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | 20-60 minutes |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Always flush pump and suction pipe with clean water at end of shift. Monitor |
 |  |  |  | suction for oversize material. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | N/A. |
 |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |
 |

heading: SYMPTOM: Discharge pipe blocked |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Discharge pipe blocked |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Sediment or gravel settled in discharge pipe if pump stopped without |
 |  |  |  | flushing. 2. Excessive slurry density causing pipe to plug. 3. Collapsed or |
 |  |  |  | kinked flexible section. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Pump pressure rises sharply (above 10 bar) with no discharge flow. Engine |
 |  |  |  | may struggle under load. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Stop pump immediately to prevent pressure damage. 2. Loosen pipe |
 |  |  |  | joints starting from pump outlet, working along pipe. 3. Locate blockage by |
 |  |  |  | finding pipe section that does not drain. 4. Clear blockage manually or flush |
 |  |  |  | with high-pressure water. 5. Inspect flexible sections for collapse. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Pipe couplings, flexible hose section if collapsed |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners, pipe wrench, high-pressure hose |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | 30-120 minutes depending on blockage location |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  |  | Level 1 for accessible blockages. Level 2 for buried or submerged pipe |
 |  |  |  | sections. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | ALWAYS flush discharge pipe with clean water at end of every shift for |
 |  |  |  | minimum 5 minutes. Monitor pump pressure -- rising pressure is early |
 |  |  |  | warning of discharge restriction. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | N/A -- operational issue. |
 |  |  |  |  |

Troubleshooting Guide   |   CONFIDENTIAL

Benamdaj Dredging Solutions Ltd.  |  Marine Engineering  |  BDS-TSG-001  |  Ver 1.0  |  Page 11  |  Uncontrolled when printed

|  |  |  |  |

heading: SYMPTOM: Winch will not hold tension -- anchor line slips |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Winch will not hold tension -- anchor line slips |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Winch brake worn or not correctly set. 2. Winch drum overloaded -- |
 |  |  |  | anchor set in wrong position relative to dredger. 3. Wire wrapped incorrectly |
 |  |  |  | on drum. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Check brake adjustment -- tighten brake if slipping. Check wire spooling on |
 |  |  |  | drum for crossovers or tangles. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Tighten winch brake adjustment. 2. Reset anchor position closer to |
 |  |  |  | vessel. 3. Re-spool wire on drum evenly. 4. If brake mechanism is |
 |  |  |  | damaged: Level 2 repair. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Winch brake pads/bands if worn |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Spanners |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | Brake adjustment: 15-30 minutes. Drum re-spool: 30-45 minutes. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for brake adjustment. Level 2 for brake component replacement. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Inspect winch brakes at every 200-hour service. Never exceed rated winch |
 |  |  |  | load. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | Normal wear -- not warranty covered. |
 |  |  |  |  |

|  |  |  |  |
 |

heading: SYMPTOM: Anchor wire snaps |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Anchor wire snaps |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Wire already damaged -- broken strands ignored. 2. Shock load from |
 |  |  |  | unexpected current or vessel movement. 3. Wire incorrectly sized for load. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Diagnostic steps |  |  | Visual inspection -- snapped wire is immediately obvious. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Stop all dredging operations immediately. 2. Deploy remaining anchors to |
 |  |  |  | hold vessel position. 3. Replace snapped wire with new wire of correct |
 |  |  |  | specification before resuming operations. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Anchor wire -- correct diameter and grade |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Wire cutters, shackle tool |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |

 |  |  | dragging -- not holding ground. 3. Current acting on vessel hull. 4. Wire |

 |  |  | current direction in anchor placement. |

heading: SYMPTOM: Battery not charging / flat battery |  |  |  |

SYMPTOM: Battery not charging / flat battery |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Alternator not charging -- belt broken or alternator failed. 2. Battery too |
 |  |  |  | old or sulphated. 3. Parasitic drain -- equipment left running when engine is |
 |  |  |  | off. 4. Corroded terminals preventing charge. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | With engine running at operating speed: check voltage across battery |
 |  |  |  | terminals -- should read 13.8-14.5 V. If less, alternator is not charging. |
 |  |  |  | Check alternator drive belt tension. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Replace alternator drive belt if broken. 2. Clean battery terminals. 3. |
 |  |  |  | Replace battery if old and not holding charge. 4. Check for equipment left on |
 |  |  |  | when engine is off. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Alternator belt, battery |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Voltmeter, spanners |

 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | Belt: 30 minutes. Battery: 30 minutes. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for belt and terminals. Level 2 for alternator replacement. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | How to prevent recurrence |  |  | Check alternator belt tension weekly. Inspect battery terminals weekly. |
 |  |  |  |  |

 |  |  |  | period. |

heading: SYMPTOM: Production rate below expected for soil type |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Production rate below expected for soil type |  |  |
 |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  | 1. Pump impeller worn -- reduced suction and flow. 2. Engine not running at |
 |  |  | rated RPM -- power limitation. 3. Suction head not at optimal depth. 4. |
 |  |  | Slurry too dilute -- mostly water. 5. Discharge pipe restriction reducing flow. |
 |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  | The company's approach: when output is not at maximum capacity, trace |
 |  |  | the cause back through the system -- engine → gearbox/PTO → pump → |
 |  |  | suction head → discharge pipe. Check each component in turn. |
 |  |  |  |

heading: SYMPTOM: Discharge distance reduced |  |  |  |
text:
SYMPTOM: Discharge distance reduced |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Worn impeller -- reduced head (pressure) capacity. 2. Partial discharge |
 |  |  |  | pipe blockage increasing friction loss. 3. Engine not at rated RPM. 4. Pump |
 |  |  |  | running at too low a speed. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Check pump pressure at rated RPM -- if pressure is below 3 bar, impeller |
 |  |  |  | may be worn. Check discharge pipe for partial blockage. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Run engine at rated speed and check pressure. 2. Flush discharge pipe |
 |  |  |  | with clean water to clear partial blockage. 3. Replace worn impeller (Level |
 |  |  |  | 2). |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | Impeller |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | Pressure gauge |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Estimated repair time |  |  | Flush: 20-30 minutes. Impeller: Level 2. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1 for flushing and RPM. Level 2 for impeller. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | How to prevent recurrence |  |  | Replace impeller on schedule. Flush discharge pipe daily at end of shift. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Warranty impact |  |  | N/A. |

 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Probable causes (most likely first) |  |  |  | 1. Suction head not close enough to dredging face. 2. Suction too high -- |
 |  |  |  | pulling surface water rather than bed material. 3. Incorrect anchor |
 |  |  |  | positioning -- dredger not over undisturbed material. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Diagnostic steps |  |  |  | Observe discharge colour -- clear or very light discharge indicates low |
 |  |  |  | density. Check suction depth and position. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
Step-by-step fix / repair |  |  |  | 1. Lower suction arm to bring head closer to seabed/riverbed. 2. Reposition |
 |  |  |  | vessel using anchor winches to bring suction over undisturbed material. 3. |
 |  |  |  | Increase engine RPM to improve suction velocity. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Parts / materials required |  |  | N/A |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Tools required |  |  | N/A |

 |  |  |  |  |

 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
 | Operator fix or specialist needed? |  |  | Level 1. |
 |  |  |  |  |
 |  |  |  |  |
How to prevent recurrence |  |  |  | Monitor discharge colour and density continuously. Reposition as soon as |
 |  |  |  | density drops. |
 |  |  |  |  |

 | Warranty impact |  |  | N/A. |

heading: 1.  Document Overview — Introduction
text:
1.  Document Overview — Introduction

This document presents 56 question-and-answer pairs derived from structured interviews conducted

with six members of staff at Benamdaj Dredging Solutions Ltd. The interviews were carried out as part

of a research project investigating the design of a Hybrid Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)

customer support system for the company.

The primary purpose of this document is to serve as a knowledge base component for the RAG system.

The Q&A pairs capture tacit operational knowledge -- the practical experience, diagnostic judgement,

and contextual understanding that experienced staff have developed through years of working with the

company's four dredger models -- and encode it in a structured, machine-readable format suitable for

retrieval.

Each Q&A pair is tagged with the following metadata: the staff persona who provided the answer, the

dredger model or models to which the answer applies, the topic category, and the retrieval priority. This

metadata enables the RAG system to filter and rank results appropriately when responding to customer

queries.

heading: 1.  Document Overview — 1.2  Knowledge Collection Methodology
text:
1.  Document Overview — 1.2  Knowledge Collection Methodology

The Q&A pairs in this document were collected through structured interviews with six members of staff

across different roles and levels of experience. Each interview followed a topic guide covering eight

subject areas relevant to customer support enquiries. Staff were asked to respond as they would if a

customer or new colleague had asked the question -- drawing on their practical knowledge rather than

formal documentation.

The interview data captures knowledge that does not appear in the formal technical manuals. Senior

staff articulate diagnostic heuristics, informal rules of thumb, and experiential understanding of

equipment behaviour under real operating conditions. This tacit knowledge is particularly valuable for

the RAG system because it closely mirrors the language that customers use when asking questions --

colloquial, problem-focused, and experience-based rather than specification-driven.

The interviews were audio-recorded with the consent of each participant, transcribed, and structured

into Q&A format by the researcher. Each answer has been lightly edited for clarity and readability

whilst preserving the original meaning and register of the interviewee's response.

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  The 10/10 uses a PTO -- a power take-off drive -- rather than a marine gearbox. The other three
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  The 10/10 uses a PTO -- a power take-off drive -- rather than a marine gearbox. The other three

models all use a marine gearbox between the engine and the pump. The PTO is simpler and cheaper to

maintain for a smaller vessel at that pump size, but it gives you less flexibility in speed control compared

to a gearbox arrangement.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: Model 4]  [Topic: A -- Equipment Knowledge]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  Can these dredgers work in saltwater, or are they freshwater only?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  Can these dredgers work in saltwater, or are they freshwater only?

A:  They can work in saltwater, but the pump casing needs to be high-chrome iron, which ours are. The

main thing is you must rinse the entire pump and suction system with fresh water after every saltwater

shift without fail. If you leave salt residue in the pump overnight it will accelerate corrosion

significantly, particularly on the impeller and wear plates.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: A -- Equipment Knowledge]  [Priority: Medium]

4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Both the 16/14 and the 14/12 can reach 22 metres depth. The 10/10 jet suction is shallower at 18

metres. The 12/10 bucket chain -- I would need to check the confirmed spec for that one; the

documentation has that figure pending confirmation from the manufacturer.

[Persona: S1 -- Senior Operator]  [Model: Model 1 / Model 2 / Model 4]  [Topic: A -- Equipment Knowledge]  [Priority:

High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What engine is in the 12/10 bucket chain dredger and what should operators know about it?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What engine is in the 12/10 bucket chain dredger and what should operators know about it?

A:  It has the Caterpillar 3408 -- a V8, about 18 litres displacement. The critical thing operators need

to know is that the 3408 uses HEUI fuel injectors, which are very sensitive to water-contaminated diesel.

You must drain the fuel water separator every week without exception on that vessel. If water gets into

the HEUI system the repair bill is serious. The Weichai engines on the other three models are more

forgiving on fuel quality.

[Persona: S4 -- Field Technician]  [Model: Model 3]  [Topic: A -- Equipment Knowledge]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What is the difference between the 16/14 and the 14/12 in practical terms?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What is the difference between the 16/14 and the 14/12 in practical terms?

A:  The 16/14 is the biggest and highest-output vessel -- up to 500 cubic metres per hour bulk in ideal

conditions, and it can discharge up to a kilometre away. The 14/12 is amphibious, meaning it can operate

on land as well as water, which makes it useful for wetland or tidal work. Its production is lower at

around 300 cubic metres per hour and discharge is 600 metres. Same engine, but different application.

You choose the 14/12 when access is the challenge and the 16/14 when volume is the priority.

[Persona: S5 -- Operations Supervisor]  [Model: Model 1 / Model 2]  [Topic: A -- Equipment Knowledge]  [Priority:

High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What auxiliary engine do all four dredgers share, and what does it power?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What auxiliary engine do all four dredgers share, and what does it power?

A:  All four vessels run a Caterpillar 3412 as the auxiliary engine. It drives the onboard generator,

which gives you 15 kVA at 240 volts. That powers the lights, instrumentation, controls, and any

ancillary equipment on the vessel. The 3412 auxiliary is reliable but it needs the same oil-change

discipline as the main engine -- do not neglect it just because it is not the dredge pump driver.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: A -- Equipment Knowledge]  [Priority: Medium]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What should an operator check first thing every morning before starting the vessel?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What should an operator check first thing every morning before starting the vessel?

A:  Oil first -- always oil first. If the oil is low and you start the engine, bearing damage can happen

within minutes and you will not know until it is too late. Then fuel level, then gearbox oil on the models

that have one. After that, check the battery voltage, inspect the hose connections for overnight

loosening, confirm the emergency stop is functional, and check that the fire extinguisher is in position.

That is the non-negotiable morning sequence.

[Persona: S1 -- Senior Operator]  [Model: All]  [Topic: B -- Daily Operations]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Minimum five minutes at idle. In cool weather or early mornings in the dry season when
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Minimum five minutes at idle. In cool weather or early mornings in the dry season when

temperatures drop, I would give it ten minutes. You want to see the oil pressure stabilise and the

temperature needle moving before you load the engine. Engaging the pump on a cold engine is one of

the fastest ways to wear the bearings prematurely.

[Persona: S1 -- Senior Operator]  [Model: All]  [Topic: B -- Daily Operations]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  The most important thing is flushing the pump before you shut the engine down. Run clean water
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  The most important thing is flushing the pump before you shut the engine down. Run clean water

through the suction for a minimum of five minutes. Slurry hardens if it sits in the pump and pipe

overnight -- I have seen operators come in the next morning to a completely blocked system. After

flushing, raise the suction ladder, reduce engine speed to idle, let it run for three to five minutes, then

shut down. Log the engine hours.

[Persona: S1 -- Senior Operator]  [Model: All]  [Topic: B -- Daily Operations]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Watch the discharge. When the material coming out of the pipe starts looking pale -- more water
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Watch the discharge. When the material coming out of the pipe starts looking pale -- more water

than slurry, almost clear -- that tells you the suction head has moved off the dredging face or you have

exhausted the material in that spot. You need to reposition before production drops further. Also watch

your engine RPM; if it starts rising without a throttle change, the pump is losing load, which means you

are pulling air or light material.

[Persona: S1 -- Senior Operator]  [Model: All]  [Topic: B -- Daily Operations]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What are the most common mistakes that new or junior operators make?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What are the most common mistakes that new or junior operators make?

A:  Three things consistently. First, not flushing the pump at end of shift -- they are tired and they skip

it. Second, not checking anchor wire tension during the shift, so the vessel drifts slowly off position and

production drops without them realising why. Third, running the engine too hard early in the shift before

it has fully warmed up, which shortens engine life. Those three account for the majority of avoidable

maintenance issues I see.

[Persona: S5 -- Operations Supervisor]  [Model: All]  [Topic: B -- Daily Operations]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  Why is flushing the pump so critical at the end of every shift?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  Why is flushing the pump so critical at the end of every shift?

A:  Because slurry -- particularly clay and fine sand -- will set almost like cement if it sits undisturbed

in the pump casing and pipe overnight. Five minutes of flushing with clean water saves you potentially

hours of downtime the next morning breaking a blockage. On the 10/10, with its narrower 10-inch

discharge pipe, this is even more critical than on the larger models. No operator should ever leave a

shift without completing the flush.

[Persona: S2 -- Junior Operator]  [Model: All]  [Topic: B -- Daily Operations]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  For the Weichai engines on Models 1, 2, and 4, you work between 1,200 and 1,800 RPM during
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  For the Weichai engines on Models 1, 2, and 4, you work between 1,200 and 1,800 RPM during

dredging. The 3408 on Model 3 is also rated for 1,800 RPM continuous in marine duty. You do not

want to run at maximum RPM continuously -- 1,500 to 1,600 is a good steady working range that

balances production with fuel consumption and engine longevity. Reserve maximum RPM for short

bursts when you need to clear a blockage or push through difficult material.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: B -- Daily Operations]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  The 10/10 has the narrowest discharge pipe of all four vessels -- 10 inches -- and the jet suction
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  The 10/10 has the narrowest discharge pipe of all four vessels -- 10 inches -- and the jet suction

system creates a very concentrated slurry stream. If you leave that sitting overnight it will set solid much

faster than it would in a larger pipe. On the 16/14 you might get away with a short flush, but on the

10/10 you must do the full five minutes every time. I would actually say seven minutes to be safe on

that vessel.

[Persona: S2 -- Junior Operator]  [Model: Model 4]  [Topic: B -- Daily Operations]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What should I check first if the pump suddenly loses suction?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What should I check first if the pump suddenly loses suction?

A:  First check that the suction head is still submerged and in contact with the dredge face -- it is easy

to drift off position without noticing. Then check for air ingress at the pipe joints; any loose connection

will let air in and break the suction. If neither of those is the cause, check the impeller. If the vessel has

done more than 200 hours since the last service, wear on the impeller is likely. A worn impeller loses

efficiency and cannot maintain suction in denser material.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: C -- Fault Diagnosis]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Black smoke almost always means the engine is not getting enough air -- it is running rich. The
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Black smoke almost always means the engine is not getting enough air -- it is running rich. The

most common cause is a clogged air filter. That is your first check and your first fix. Replace the air

filter and see if the smoke clears. If it does not, the injectors may be over-fuelling or you have a boost

issue. But start with the air filter; it accounts for the majority of black smoke cases we see.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: C -- Fault Diagnosis]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  All engine knock should be treated as serious. There is no such thing as a minor knock -- any
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  All engine knock should be treated as serious. There is no such thing as a minor knock -- any

knocking sound from the engine means metal is moving in a way it should not, whether that is a piston,

a bearing, or a valve. Stop the engine immediately, check the oil, and do not restart until you have

investigated. If you restart and the knock returns, shut down again and call a specialist. Operating

through a knock is how you turn a repairable fault into an engine replacement.

[Persona: S4 -- Field Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: C -- Fault Diagnosis]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Stop the pump immediately -- do not try to force material through under pressure as that can blow
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Stop the pump immediately -- do not try to force material through under pressure as that can blow

a joint. Start from the pump outlet end and work along the pipe, loosening joints until you locate where

the blockage is. Clear it manually or flush with high-pressure water. Check all the flexible sections of

the pipe as those are the most common blockage points, particularly at bends. Once clear, flush the

entire line with clean water before resuming dredging to confirm the path is open.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: C -- Fault Diagnosis]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  The engine temperature alarm comes on during operation -- what is the correct response?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  The engine temperature alarm comes on during operation -- what is the correct response?

A:  Stop dredging immediately and reduce to idle. If the alarm is accompanied by a temperature reading

above 105 degrees Celsius, shut the engine down straight away. Allow at least ten minutes cool-down

before opening any cooling system components. Check the fan belt, the coolant level, and the air intake

to make sure nothing is blocking it. Also check whether the ventilation around the engine bay is

restricted. Do not restart until the root cause is identified.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: C -- Fault Diagnosis]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Maximum urgency. Stop the engine immediately -- do not wait, do not finish the pass, stop now.
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Maximum urgency. Stop the engine immediately -- do not wait, do not finish the pass, stop now.

Low oil pressure means the engine bearings are running dry or close to it, and bearing failure can happen

in under a minute at operating speed. Once stopped, check the dipstick. If the oil level is correct and the

pressure warning persists on restart, do not run the engine again until a specialist has checked the oil

pump and relief valve.

[Persona: S4 -- Field Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: C -- Fault Diagnosis]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  The engine keeps stalling when load is applied -- what is likely causing it?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  The engine keeps stalling when load is applied -- what is likely causing it?

A:  Three main candidates. First, fuel starvation -- a blocked fuel filter or air in the fuel system. That

is the most common. Second, the idle speed is set too low for the ambient temperature, so the engine

cannot sustain load at idle. Third, water contamination in the fuel, which is particularly relevant on the

12/10 with its sensitive HEUI injector system. Change the fuel filter first, then bleed the fuel system if

the stalling continues.

[Persona: S4 -- Field Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: C -- Fault Diagnosis]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  On the bucket chain, an overheating gearbox is usually either low gearbox oil or excessive back-
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  On the bucket chain, an overheating gearbox is usually either low gearbox oil or excessive back-

pressure from the discharge system creating additional load. Top up the gearbox oil to the correct level

first -- that alone resolves it in most cases. If the oil is at the correct level, check whether there is a

partial blockage in the discharge increasing resistance. Allow the gearbox to cool for 20 minutes before

resuming operation.

[Persona: S4 -- Field Technician]  [Model: Model 3]  [Topic: C -- Fault Diagnosis]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Work through it systematically. First, check engine RPM -- are you running at the same speed as
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Work through it systematically. First, check engine RPM -- are you running at the same speed as

yesterday? Second, check pump pressure -- is it within normal range? Third, has the dredge face

changed? Harder material or a different depth may explain lower output. Fourth, is the discharge pipe

clear? A partial blockage will reduce flow without stopping it completely. Fifth, check the impeller if

you are approaching or past the 200-hour service mark. One of those five will usually be the cause.

[Persona: S1 -- Senior Operator]  [Model: All]  [Topic: C -- Fault Diagnosis]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  White smoke is a warning sign of water or coolant entering the combustion chamber. The most
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  White smoke is a warning sign of water or coolant entering the combustion chamber. The most

likely cause is a leaking head gasket. Stop the engine and allow it to cool. Do not continue operating --

if coolant is entering the cylinders you can cause severe internal damage very quickly. This is a job for

the workshop; a pressure test of the cooling system will confirm the diagnosis. Do not confuse white

smoke with steam on a cold morning start -- steam on startup for the first minute or two is normal.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: C -- Fault Diagnosis]  [Priority: High]

4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  The pump seal is leaking -- is that always a problem?

Page 8  |  BDS-FAQ-001  |  For Research Use -- MSc AI & Data Science, University of Wolverhampton

BDS-FAQ-001  |  Staff Interview FAQs  |  Benamdaj Dredging Solutions Ltd.

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  A small amount of leakage from the gland packing is normal and expected -- two to three drops
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  A small amount of leakage from the gland packing is normal and expected -- two to three drops

per minute is the acceptable range and actually helps to lubricate and cool the packing. What you are

looking for is a continuous steady flow or dripping. If it reaches that level, tighten the gland packing

nuts a quarter turn and monitor it. If tightening does not reduce it within a shift, the packing needs

replacing. That is a Level 2 maintenance task -- do not keep dredging with a significant seal leak.

[Persona: S6 -- Workshop Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: C -- Fault Diagnosis]  [Priority: Medium]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Stop all dredging operations immediately. Deploy the remaining anchors to hold position. Do not
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Stop all dredging operations immediately. Deploy the remaining anchors to hold position. Do not

attempt to continue dredging with fewer anchor points -- you will lose control of the vessel's position

and risk equipment damage or collision. Replace the wire before resuming any dredging. Inspect the

other wires for wear or fraying at the same time, because if one has gone the others may be close to

failure.

[Persona: S5 -- Operations Supervisor]  [Model: All]  [Topic: C -- Fault Diagnosis]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Engine oil change, every 200 hours, without exception. Everything else follows from that. The
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Engine oil change, every 200 hours, without exception. Everything else follows from that. The

engine is the most expensive component on the vessel and oil condition directly determines its life. We

use a more conservative interval than the OEM recommendation because our operating conditions --

tropical heat, high humidity, heavy continuous loading in abrasive environments -- are significantly

more demanding than the standard conditions manufacturers base their intervals on. Missing an oil

change by even 20 or 30 hours can make a measurable difference to engine wear rates.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: D -- Maintenance Practice]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What engine oil is used across the fleet and why that grade?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What engine oil is used across the fleet and why that grade?

A:  We use Total Ruby X across all four vessels. It is a high-performance diesel engine oil suited to the

high-temperature, high-load conditions of continuous marine dredging. Do not substitute with a

different brand or grade without checking with the chief technician -- the viscosity and additive

package matter for the specific clearances in these engines, particularly the 3408 with its HEUI injector

system.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: D -- Maintenance Practice]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  How many grease points are there and how should they be serviced?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  How many grease points are there and how should they be serviced?

A:  There are seven grease points across the vessel. They should all be greased at the start of every

operating day. Apply two to three shots of grease gun per point -- no more. Over-greasing is as harmful

as under-greasing because excess grease attracts grit and abrasive particles, which then work their way

into the bearing surfaces. Use a good quality EP2 grease or the grade specified in the maintenance

schedule.

[Persona: S6 -- Workshop Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: D -- Maintenance Practice]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Engine oil and filter change, fuel filter replacement, gearbox oil check and top-up or change
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Engine oil and filter change, fuel filter replacement, gearbox oil check and top-up or change

depending on condition, inspection of pump wear plates and impeller clearance, cutter head inspection

on the applicable models, greasing all points, checking and tensioning all fasteners, inspecting the

anchor wires, checking electrical connections and battery, and logging the hours and any observations.

It takes a full working day if done properly. Do not cut it short.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: D -- Maintenance Practice]  [Priority: High]

4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  How do I know when the impeller needs replacing rather than just adjusting?

BDS-FAQ-001  |  Staff Interview FAQs  |  Benamdaj Dredging Solutions Ltd.

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Three indicators. First, production has dropped by 20 per cent or more at the same RPM compared
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Three indicators. First, production has dropped by 20 per cent or more at the same RPM compared

to a baseline reading. Second, measured impeller diameter is more than 10 per cent below the new

specification. Third, you can hear a rougher sound from the pump during operation -- a slight grinding

or uneven noise that was not there before. Any one of those warrants inspection. All three together

means replace without delay.

[Persona: S1 -- Senior Operator]  [Model: All]  [Topic: D -- Maintenance Practice]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  We change oil every 200 operating hours. The original equipment manufacturer's recommendation
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  We change oil every 200 operating hours. The original equipment manufacturer's recommendation

is longer, but we operate in conditions that accelerate oil degradation -- high ambient temperature in

Nigeria, continuous heavy loading, dusty or humid environments near riverbanks. We have found from

experience that waiting for the OEM interval results in oil that is significantly degraded by the time it

is changed. The additional cost of more frequent oil changes is far less than the cost of premature engine

wear.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: D -- Maintenance Practice]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  How should the fuel water separator be maintained, particularly on the 12/10?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  How should the fuel water separator be maintained, particularly on the 12/10?

A:  The fuel water separator should be drained at the start of each shift as part of the pre-start checklist.

On the 12/10 with the Caterpillar 3408, I would say weekly at an absolute minimum -- the HEUI

injectors on that engine are genuinely sensitive to water-contaminated diesel in a way the Weichai

engines are not. If you see water collecting in the separator faster than normal, investigate the fuel

supply -- there may be condensation in the tank or a contaminated delivery batch.

[Persona: S4 -- Field Technician]  [Model: Model 3]  [Topic: D -- Maintenance Practice]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  How often is the hull survey carried out and what does it involve?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  How often is the hull survey carried out and what does it involve?

A:  The hull survey is annual. The vessel is dry-docked or hauled out for inspection of the underwater

hull, sacrificial anodes, shaft seals, propulsion fittings, and any structural integrity checks. It is a

statutory requirement for commercial vessels and also a practical necessity -- the underwater condition

of the hull affects stability and performance in ways you cannot assess from above the waterline. Do

not defer it.

[Persona: S5 -- Operations Supervisor]  [Model: All]  [Topic: D -- Maintenance Practice]  [Priority: Medium]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Plan on 70 per cent of the rated maximum as your working figure -- so 350 to 400 cubic metres
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Plan on 70 per cent of the rated maximum as your working figure -- so 350 to 400 cubic metres

per hour for the 16/14 under good conditions. The rated 500 cubic metres per hour is achievable but

represents ideal circumstances: the right material, optimal depth, fresh anchor positions, engine at full

power. In practice, material variability, repositioning time, and engine thermal management all reduce

the achievable average. Operators who plan on 70 per cent and occasionally exceed it are more reliable

than those who promise maximum output and consistently miss it.

[Persona: S1 -- Senior Operator]  [Model: Model 1]  [Topic: E -- Performance]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  A few reasons combine. The easy, surface material is gone by afternoon -- you are working deeper
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  A few reasons combine. The easy, surface material is gone by afternoon -- you are working deeper

or in harder material that was left untouched for a reason. The engine and hydraulics run hotter in

afternoon heat, which requires more conservative RPM management. And honestly, operator attention

drifts as fatigue sets in -- anchor repositioning gets delayed, the suction head is not repositioned as

Page 10  |  BDS-FAQ-001  |  For Research Use -- MSc AI & Data Science, University of Wolverhampton

BDS-FAQ-001  |  Staff Interview FAQs  |  Benamdaj Dredging Solutions Ltd.

promptly, and those small delays compound into lower production figures. Early morning, first two

hours of the shift, is typically when you get your best output.

[Persona: S5 -- Operations Supervisor]  [Model: All]  [Topic: E -- Performance]  [Priority: Medium]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What soil types are unsuitable for these dredgers?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What soil types are unsuitable for these dredgers?

A:  Hard rock and stiff overconsolidated clay are beyond the capability of cutter suction and jet suction

operation. Coarse gravel above about 150 millimetres particle size can damage the impeller and should

be avoided. The bucket chain on the 12/10 can handle somewhat harder material, but it also has its

limits. When in doubt, a soil investigation report before committing the vessel will save significant

damage costs.

[Persona: S3 -- Chief Technician]  [Model: All]  [Topic: E -- Performance]  [Priority: Medium]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  How do I diagnose low discharge density -- the output looks mostly water?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  How do I diagnose low discharge density -- the output looks mostly water?

A:  Three most likely causes in order. First, the suction arm has drifted away from the dredge face --

lower it and reposition. Second, you have exhausted the material in the immediate area and need to

move the vessel. Third, the engine is not at rated RPM so the pump is not generating enough velocity

to carry the material. Check your RPM first because that is the quickest check, then assess position.

Low-density discharge is wasted operating time and fuel.

[Persona: S1 -- Senior Operator]  [Model: All]  [Topic: E -- Performance]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What is the fire procedure on board?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What is the fire procedure on board?

A:  Alert the crew immediately. Activate the emergency stop to shut down fuel systems. Use the nearest

fire extinguisher. The rule we apply is the 60-second rule -- if the fire is not under control within 60

seconds of first attack, every person leaves the vessel immediately. No equipment is worth a life. Once

everyone is off, call for emergency assistance. Do not re-board until the fire is confirmed out and the

vessel is safe.

[Persona: S5 -- Operations Supervisor]  [Model: All]  [Topic: F -- Safety]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Shout man overboard immediately so everyone on the vessel is aware. Throw the life ring -- it is
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Shout man overboard immediately so everyone on the vessel is aware. Throw the life ring -- it is

kept at the bow of every vessel. Stop the engine or reduce to the minimum speed immediately to prevent

propeller risk. Keep eyes on the person in the water at all times -- designate one crew member to do

nothing else but watch them. Deploy a diver or boarding ladder as soon as it is safe to do so. Call for

emergency assistance if the person cannot be recovered quickly.

[Persona: S5 -- Operations Supervisor]  [Model: All]  [Topic: F -- Safety]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What PPE is required on the vessel?
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — Q:  What PPE is required on the vessel?

A:  At minimum: life jacket at all times when on the open deck or working near the water, safety boots

with steel toe caps, hard hat when working near the cutter or ladder systems, ear protection when in the

engine bay, and gloves when handling wires or hoses. The life jacket requirement is non-negotiable --

no exceptions for experienced operators, no exceptions in calm weather. One slip on a wet deck is all it

takes.

[Persona: S5 -- Operations Supervisor]  [Model: All]  [Topic: F -- Safety]  [Priority: High]

heading: 4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Stop operating immediately. Reduce to idle if it is safe to do so, or shut down outright. Do not
text:
4.  Q&A Pairs by Category — A:  Stop operating immediately. Reduce to idle if it is safe to do so, or shut down outright. Do not

continue dredging hoping the sound will resolve itself -- it will not, and continued operation will make

the damage worse. Investigate the source before restarting. If the sound is from the engine, check oil
